data.title: OFG Bancorp outlines low single-digit loan growth for 2026 while advancing digital strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OFG Bancorp (OFG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • José Fernández, President, CEO & Chairman, reported a 17% year-over-year increase in diluted earnings per share for the quarter on 2% growth in total core revenues, attributing this to disciplined operations and a favorable tax benefit. Asset quality and credit metrics were described as sound, with increased commercial loans and strong acceptance of the Libre and Elite deposit accounts.
  • Fernández highlighted that "one of our biggest strategic and financial accomplishments of 2025 was the progress we made with our Digital First strategy" and described the launch of an omnichannel platform and intelligent banking model that delivers tailored insights to customers.
  • He stated that "capital continued to grow, and we repurchased $40 million of common shares in the fourth quarter," emphasizing robust business activity in Puerto Rico and resilience among businesses and consumers.
  • Maritza Arizmendi, Chief Financial Officer, stated, “Core revenues totaled $185 million, an increase of $1.4 million. Total interest income was $197 million, a decrease of $3 million... Total interest expense was $44 million, a decrease of $1 million... Total banking and financial service revenues were $33 million, an increase of $3.4 million.”
  • Arizmendi noted a loss of $1.1 million in other income due to “$6.1 million for accelerated amortization of technology-related assets,” offset by gains on sales of nonperforming loans and real estate. Noninterest expenses rose $8.5 million, reflecting performance-based advisory costs and business rightsizing.

Outlook

  • Arizmendi projected total noninterest expense for 2026 to be between $380 million to $385 million.
  • She indicated that "loans to continue to grow in low single digits" and expects "retail and commercial deposits to increase with Libre+, Elite, Oriental Biz and our digital offerings driving customer growth."
  • Net interest margin is expected to range between 4.95% to 5.05% in 2026, factoring in funding cost changes and partial exit of government deposits.
  • The effective tax rate for 2026 is estimated around 23%, excluding discrete items.
  • Fernández reiterated a focus on capital return through buybacks, saying "we will continue to be very intentional there. We certainly will also look at the dividend."

Financial Results

  • Core revenues reached $185 million, up $1.4 million sequentially, while total interest income declined $3 million. Total interest expense was $44 million, down $1 million from the third quarter.
  • Total banking and financial service revenues increased by $3.4 million, mainly due to higher wealth management revenues. Other income showed a loss of $1.1 million, reflecting accelerated amortization costs, though partially offset by gains on asset sales.
  • Noninterest expenses totaled $105 million, up $8.5 million, attributed to advisory costs and technology investments.
  • Tangible book value was reported at $29.96 per share, with an efficiency ratio of 56.7%, return on average assets at 1.81%, and return on average tangible common equity at 17.2%.
  • Average loan balances rose slightly, with commercial loans growing to $3.5 billion, representing 43% of the loan book. Core deposit balances were $9.9 billion, up almost 1% from the third quarter.
  • Provision for credit losses was $31.9 million, up $4 million, driven by increased loan volume and specific reserves, notably a Puerto Rico telecommunications commercial loan.

Q&A

  • Kelly Motta, KBW, asked about elevated provisions and charge-offs, with Cesar Ortiz-Marcano, Chief Risk Officer, explaining, “the charge-offs that you're looking in the quarter are the result of a sale that we performed... that released $17 million in nonperforming loans during the quarter,” along with a new nonaccrual telecom loan.
  • Motta also inquired about loan growth outlook; Fernández indicated auto balances are expected to decline 2%-3%, commercial loans to rise 5%-6%, and overall loan growth to remain in low single digits.
  • On expense control, Motta questioned confidence in flat noninterest expenses; Arizmendi emphasized ongoing technology investments and workforce efficiencies, noting, “we are harvesting certain efficiencies... we will start seeing some of that savings and the 2027 and 2028, we see savings to accelerate.”
  • Arren Cyganovich, Truist, asked about 2026 strategic initiatives; Fernández said the focus would shift more to commercial banking and further translating digital strategies to that segment.
  • On capital return, Fernández reiterated the priority of buybacks due to current valuation.
  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde, sought clarity on loan yield and NIM; Arizmendi cited the impact of Fed rate cuts and asset sensitivity as key drivers. Fernández added improved credit quality in auto lending contributed to yield stabilization.
  • Timur Braziler, Wells Fargo, addressed credit normalization; Arizmendi stated a 1% net charge-off rate is likely to persist, while allowance builds included a specific telecom loan reserve.
  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler, explored competitive response to Libre and Elite products; Fernández said, “there's no need to be -- to have a price response because we're not paying high yields... It's not about the rate only. It's about the... value proposition.” He disclosed 75% of Libre accounts are new customers, 40% are 29 years or younger.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern about elevated provisions, loan growth drivers, expense controls, and margin outlook. The tone was probing but not overtly negative, with several follow-ups for clarification on strategy and expense discipline.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, frequently emphasizing asset quality, technology-driven efficiencies, and strategic positioning. Fernández used phrases like "we feel more confident in our expense ranges in '26, for sure" and "we will continue to work hard to bring additional expense reductions."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the overall sentiment remained positive, with increased focus on margin pressures and expense control but sustained confidence from management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s guidance for loan growth shifted from 5%-6% annual growth to "low single digits" for 2026, reflecting heightened caution amid auto loan moderation.
  • Expense guidance for 2026 is more focused on flat year-over-year performance, with a longer-term view toward efficiency gains in 2027-2028.
  • The strategic focus is moving more toward commercial banking, as opposed to prior quarters’ emphasis on retail and digital.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from deposit costs and digital adoption to concerns about provision levels, charge-offs, and the sustainability of expense controls.
  • Management’s confidence in technology-driven efficiencies and capital return remains strong, though there is acknowledgment of competitive pressures and funding cost changes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated provision for credit losses, notably due to a specific telecommunications loan, was highlighted as a challenge.
  • Seasonality in auto portfolio charge-offs and delinquencies was discussed, with expectations for normalization in early 2026.
  • Management acknowledged intensifying competition for deposits, especially from U.S. credit unions and targeted CD offerings, but stated their strategy is based on value-added products rather than higher rates.
  • The need to manage the impact of government deposit exits and replacement with higher-cost wholesale funding was noted.
  • Ongoing global macroeconomic and political uncertainties were referenced as factors to monitor for potential impact on Puerto Rico’s economic environment.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored OFG Bancorp’s continued progress in digital banking, robust capital return initiatives, and disciplined expense management. Loan growth is expected in the low single digits for 2026, with a strategic pivot toward commercial banking and technology-led efficiencies. Asset quality remains sound, though elevated provisions related to a single telecom loan and ongoing competitive deposit dynamics will be closely managed in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Capri Holdings gains after TD Cowen says the Michael Kors transformation is working
data.description:
TD Cowen turned bullish on Capri Holdings (CPRI) as it boosted its rating to Buy from Neutral. 

Analyst Oliver Chen and his team pointed to an upgrade to a sharper price point strategy from Capri (CPRI) combined with desirable trend-right styles. The firm also sees substantial operating margin expansion potential this year and financial deleverage opportunities. Progress with the Michael Kors transformation in terms of store closures, outlet remerchandising, handbag pricing architecture, and marketing repositioning is also considered a positive.

It was also highlighted that the new Michael Kors products appear to be on trend, and the company is embracing data to drive personalization.

TD Cowen raised its price target on Capri (CPRI) to $32 from $25 based on a 16X multiple to the two-year out earnings estimate. "We are encouraged by improved product at Michael Kors, strategic pricing architecture aimed to increase full-price sell-through and average unit retail, store rationalization, and outlet elevation effort," wrote Chen.

Shares of Capri Holdings (CPRI) were up 2.9% in Thursday afternoon trading.

data.title: Rick Rieder gains noticeable momentum in Fed Chair race, according to Polymarket
data.description:
Rick Rieder is gaining noticeable momentum in the race to become the next Federal Reserve Chairman, according to data from prediction market Polymarket.

The question—“Who will President Trump nominate as Fed Chair?”—has seen Rieder’s prospects rise sharply over the past month. 

Currently, Rieder holds a 32% chance in the market, trailing only Kevin Warsh, who leads with 44%. The shift is particularly notable when compared with the start of the year, when Rieder’s odds stood at just 4.9%, while Warsh commanded 32%.

This surge reflects growing confidence among market participants in Rieder’s candidacy, signaling a potential shift in expectations for the Fed’s leadership. 

Other figures in the running include Christopher Waller at 14%, Kevin Hassett at 6%, Scott Bessent at 3%, and both Judy Shelton and Michelle Bowman at 1% each. The data underscores a dynamic and evolving landscape in the lead-up to the nomination, with Rieder emerging as a significant contender alongside Warsh.

Market Tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Bank of America, Citi consider new credit cards with 10% rate - report
data.description:
After President Donald Trump asked Congress to pass legislation to cap credit card interest rates at 10% for a year, two large U.S. banks are considering new products that may address the administration's concerns about affordability, according to a media report on Thursday.

Separately, Bank of America (BAC) and Citigroup (C) are exploring cards with a 10% rate as a solution, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

Many issuers, including BofA (BAC) and Citi (C), already offer introductory rates as low as 0% for a period of time, the report noted.

Soon after Trump floated the idea of a 10% rate cap, several bank CEOs pushed back, saying the limit would severely impair consumers' access to credit, which, in turn, would reduce consumer spending.

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) chief Jamie Dimon said, "Specifically, people will lose access to credit, like on a very, very extensive and broad basis, especially the people who need it the most."

However, some bank executives have publicly agreed with Trump's focus on affordability. For example, Citi (C) CEO Jane Fraser said the president is right to focus on affordability, but a credit card interest rate cap would not help. "You would not be able to provide credit and access to credit for people who need it the most," she said in a CNBC interview on Tuesday.

Bank of America (BAC) has been discussing the issue with the administration, CEO Brian Moynihan said in a Bloomberg Television interview on Thursday. "We're all for affordability in financial products," he said. The bank already offers a no-frills credit card with a lower rate, but it's not as low as 10%, he said.

The issue is, "To get credit losses down to the point where you can afford that rate, you can never have a charge-off," Moynihan said in the Bloomberg interview.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Hudbay signs on new Japanese partner for Manitoba metals exploration
data.description:
Hudbay Minerals (HBM) +1.9% in Thursday's trading after saying it secured a new Japanese funding partner to bolster its exploration in northern Manitoba of properties that in the past produced both base and precious metals.

The miner said it granted Japanese government agency Japan Organization for Metals & Energy Security an option to buy a 10% stake in three projects located within trucking distance of Hudbay's (HBM) processing facilities in Flin Flon, Manitoba; to exercise the option, the agency agreed to fund at least C$6M (US$4.3M) for exploration over approximately three years, with Hudbay acting as the operator.

Hudbay (HBM) said the agreement represents an amendment of a deal it struck in 2024 with Japanese conglomerate Marubeni, which won the right to buy a 20% interest in the three projects, provided it funds at least C$12M in exploration spending.

Upon successful completion of earn-in obligations by JOGMEC and Marubeni and if both parties elect to exercise their respective options, a three-party joint venture would be formed to hold the selected projects with Hudbay (HBM) acting as operator, owning a 70% interest, with Marubeni holding 20% and JOGMEC owning the remaining 10% interest.

data.title: CVB Financial outlines continued loan pipeline strength and targets stable growth into 2026 amid Heritage merger progress
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CVB Financial Corp. (CVBF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Dave Brager, President and CEO, reported "net earnings of $55 million or $0.40 per share, representing our 195th consecutive quarter of profitability, which equates to more than 48 years" and announced a $0.20 per share dividend for the quarter. He highlighted a return on average tangible common equity of 14.4% and a return on average assets of 1.40%. Pretax income grew by $5.4 million quarter-over-quarter, primarily due to growth in net interest income. Brager also noted, "Loan originations were approximately 70% higher in 2025 than 2024, and the fourth quarter production was approximately 15% higher than the third quarter of 2025. Our loan pipelines remain strong going into 2026."
  • Brager discussed the pending merger with Heritage Bank of Commerce, stating, "We incurred $1.6 million of acquisition expense related to the pending merger with Heritage Bank of Commerce."
  • Brager reported that total loans at December 31, 2025, were $8.7 billion, a $228 million or 2.7% increase from the end of the third quarter, with growth across nearly all loan categories, especially dairy and livestock, which saw a $139 million increase.
  • Allen Nicholson, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Net interest income was $122.7 million in the fourth quarter of 2025. This compares to $115.6 million in the third quarter of 2025 and $110.4 million in the fourth quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • Brager indicated that loan pipelines remain strong and expressed optimism: "I'm cautiously optimistic, maybe even positive and optimistic about 2026 so far."
  • Nicholson described the economic forecast as modestly changed from the previous quarter, with loss rate assumptions for C&I loans experiencing a negative impact: "Real GDP is forecasted to stay below 1.5% through 2027 and not reach 2% until 2029. The unemployment rate is forecasted to reach 5% by the beginning of 2026 and remain above 5% through 2028."
  • Regarding the Heritage merger, Brager maintained, "We still anticipate second quarter close and a second quarter systems conversion."

Financial Results

  • The company reported net earnings of $55 million or $0.40 per share, up from $52.6 million or $0.38 per share in Q3 2025.
  • Net interest income grew by $7 million quarter-over-quarter, and by $12.2 million year-over-year.
  • Noninterest income was $11.2 million, down $1.8 million from Q3, primarily due to lower bank-owned life insurance income and a decline in other income following a smaller loss on sale of investments and the absence of a legal settlement.
  • The company collected $3.2 million of interest on a nonperforming loan payoff but incurred a $2.8 million loss on sale of investment securities and $1.6 million in acquisition expenses.
  • Loan growth was broad-based, with notable increases in dairy and livestock, C&I, CRE, and SBA 504 loans. Loan originations in Q4 had average yields of approximately 6.25%.
  • Deposit balances averaged $12.6 billion, with seasonal declines in noninterest-bearing deposits and increases in interest-bearing deposits.
  • Cost of deposits and repos decreased to 86 basis points from 90 basis points in Q3.
  • The allowance for credit losses was $77 million, down from $79 million in Q3, representing 0.89% of gross loans.
  • Shareholders' equity rose to $2.3 billion, and 1.96 million shares were repurchased during the quarter at an average price of $18.80.

Q&A

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked about seasonality and changes in interest-bearing deposits. Brager responded, "People pay bonuses, accrue for taxes, do different things. So I don't really think there was any major change... it just was normal seasonality."
  • Clark: Inquired about nondairy and livestock loan growth. Brager commented, "Loan pipelines remain strong... I'm cautiously optimistic, maybe even positive and optimistic about 2026 so far."
  • Clark: Requested an update on the Heritage deal. Brager stated, "Everything is going well... We still anticipate second quarter close and a second quarter systems conversion."
  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about deposit competition and betas. Brager explained, "We're not a bank that goes out and offers the highest rate on our deposit accounts."
  • Feaster: Questioned loan growth competition. Brager said, "It's really more pricing than credit. We're not going to do something that we wouldn't do from a credit underwriting perspective."
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Asked about expense growth. Nicholson confirmed, "There were some adjustments to our private bonus share accruals that elevated the expense quarter-over-quarter."
  • Terrell: Inquired about margin normalization. Nicholson stated, "It will take a little time for both cash flows and the security book to reprice as well as the loan book reprice."
  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: Asked about loan yields. Nicholson attributed the improvement to mix changes and new production.
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Asked about noninterest-bearing deposit flows. Brager responded, "No, there was no loss of relationships... just normal stuff."
  • Timothy Coffey, Janney: Asked about loan modifications. Brager explained, "There's a lot of different reasons they can fall into that..."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on core deposit dynamics, loan growth sustainability, and the competitive landscape without notable skepticism, and sought clarity on merger integration and expense trends.
  • Management maintained a consistently confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with Brager noting, "I'm cautiously optimistic, maybe even positive and optimistic about 2026 so far."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management sentiment shifted slightly more positive, especially regarding loan pipelines, merger progress, and overall financial health.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language reflected increased optimism about loan pipelines and merger integration, compared to a more cautious tone in Q3.
  • Strategic focus expanded with the pending Heritage merger, while maintaining loan growth and credit quality as priorities.
  • Key metrics such as net interest income, net earnings, and loan balances improved sequentially.
  • Management’s confidence strengthened, with more direct statements of optimism regarding 2026 outlook and integration benefits.
  • Analyst tone shifted from cautious inquiry in Q3 to deeper exploration of competitive dynamics and future expense management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Intense rate competition for quality loans remains, with Brager noting, "rate competition for the quality of loans we compete for continues to be intense."
  • Seasonal declines in noninterest-bearing deposits were observed, alongside increases in interest-bearing balances.
  • Economic forecasts anticipate GDP below 1.5% through 2027 and unemployment above 5% into 2028, presenting ongoing macroeconomic headwinds.
  • Management is monitoring loan payoffs and paydowns, acknowledging some risk if competitors offer lower rates on resets.
  • Integration of Heritage Bank of Commerce presents execution risk, particularly in balance sheet restructuring and cultural alignment, though management described the process as on track.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized a strong finish to 2025 with robust loan pipelines, healthy credit quality, and the expectation of positive momentum into 2026, supported by continued progress on the Heritage Bank of Commerce merger and sustained focus on operational discipline and relationship-based banking. The company signaled cautious optimism for stable growth, disciplined expense management, and further opportunities to deepen customer relationships in an evolving competitive and economic environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Reliance Global to change ticker symbol to EZRA from RELI
data.description:
Reliance Global Group (RELI) on Thursday said its ticker symbol on the NASDAQ Capital Market will change to EZRA beginning Monday, January 26.

The ticker symbol change is intended to better reflect the company's strategic evolution, including the formation of EZRA International Group, a new division focused on acquiring controlling stakes.

EZRA International's initial emphasis will be on businesses operating across sectors such as cybersecurity, artificial intelligence and data analytics, FinTech and InsurTech, and MedTech and digital health.

data.title: Google's AI mode to generate responses with personal context by connecting to Gmail, Photos
data.description:
• Tech giant Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) said Thursday it is expanding its “Personal Intelligence” feature into AI Mode and allowing users to connect their Gmail and Photos to generate more custom responses based on their emails and images.
  • Google said that AI Mode will not train directly from an individual's Gmail inbox or photo library but will instead learn from specific prompts and responses generated.
  • The ability to connect AI Mode to Gmail and Google Photos is rolling out as a “Labs” feature, the company said.
  • Connecting Gmail and Google Photos is strictly opt-in, the company said.
  • Source: Blog

data.title: Texas Capital projects mid- to high single-digit revenue growth and $265M–$290M noninterest income for 2026 while expanding fee income streams
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Texas Capital Bancshares (TCBI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert Holmes described 2025 as a “defining year” for Texas Capital, emphasizing the completion of the firm’s transformation and the achievement of key targets: “In the third quarter, we achieved our stated financial targets, marking completion of our transformation and delivering the largest organic profitability improvement of any commercial bank exceeding $20 billion in assets over the past 2 decades.” Holmes highlighted a 1.2% ROAA in Q4 and noted that “full year adjusted ROAA of 1.04% represents a 30 basis point improvement versus 2024 and signals a fundamental improvement of our earnings power.”
  • Holmes reported record adjusted total revenue of $1.3 billion, adjusted net income to common stockholders of $314 million, adjusted EPS of $6.80, and adjusted pre-provision net revenue of $489 million. He added, “We achieved record tangible common equity to tangible assets of 10.56% and record tangible book value per share of $75.25.”
  • The CEO also stated, “Fee income areas of focus generated $192 million in 2025 with substantial growth opportunity ahead.” He pointed out that the business is now positioned for “consistent execution and compounding returns,” driven by balance sheet growth, operating leverage, and expanding fee income.
  • CFO Matt Scurlock stated, “Fourth quarter results capped a record year with broad-based improvements across all key metrics. Our increasingly durable business model, uniquely positioned to deliver high-quality client outcomes, is translating into sustainably strong financial performance.” Scurlock highlighted a year-over-year quarterly revenue increase of 15% to $327.5 million and record full year net interest income of $1.03 billion. He noted, “Quarterly adjusted noninterest expense decreased 2% or $4.2 million to $186.4 million, benefiting from continued expense realignment.”

Outlook

  • Management projected mid- to high single-digit total revenue growth for 2026, driven by client adoption and fee income expansion. Scurlock stated, “We anticipate total revenue growth in the mid- to high single-digit range, driven by industry-leading client adoption and continued growth in our fee income areas of focus with full year noninterest revenue expected to reach $265 million to $290 million.”
  • Expected noninterest expense growth is in the mid-single digits, reflecting increased compensation and targeted expansion. Provision outlook for 2026 is 35 to 40 basis points of average loans held for investment (excluding mortgage finance), moderated due to economic uncertainty.
  • Management’s language points to disciplined execution and a focus on scaling investments to further drive profitability.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted net income to common was $94.6 million for the quarter, or $2.08 per share, up 45% year-over-year. Full year adjusted net income to common was $313.8 million or $6.80 per share, a 53% improvement over 2024.
  • Full year adjusted total revenue reached $1.26 billion, up 13% year-over-year, with net interest income growing 14% to $1.03 billion. Adjusted fee-based revenue grew 9% to $229 million, a third consecutive year of record fee income.
  • Commercial loan balances expanded $254 million or 8% annualized during Q4; gross loans held for investment increased $1.6 billion or 7% year-over-year to $24.1 billion, mainly from commercial loans.
  • The allowance for credit loss including off-balance sheet reserves stood at $333 million, near all-time highs, with the credit loss ratio at 1.82% of total loans held for investment (excluding mortgage finance).
  • Tangible common equity to tangible assets finished at 10.6%, with tangible book value per share increasing 13.44% year-over-year to $75.25.

Q&A

  • Wood Lay, KBW: Asked about the investment banking pipeline and fee pacing for 2026. Holmes replied, “IV transaction volume was up about 40%. The fees were much more granular. So people like you and others would suggest that's healthy, better earnings stream.” Scurlock guided investment banking fees of $160 million to $175 million for 2026 and total noninterest income of $265 million to $290 million.
  • Lay also asked about capital deployment and buybacks. Scurlock responded, “Pushing CET1 up 75 basis points to 12.13%, while growing loans, $1.6 billion or 7%, buying back 5% of the company for 114% of prior month tangible, and building tangible book value per share by 13.44%. We’re obviously pretty pleased with how we utilize shareholders' capital for their benefit in 2025.”
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about expense outlook and hiring. Scurlock said, “We are highly focused on leveraging the material previous investments that we've made by expanding capabilities and adding targeted coverage with the 2026 expense guide, continue to heavily feature growth in salaries and benefits with select increase in technology.”
  • Jackson Singleton, Autonomous: Asked about credit trends and provision guidance. Scurlock explained, “We did experience modest linked quarter increase in special mentioned loans, which…was tied exclusively to a handful of multifamily properties that are experiencing net operating income pressure, just given required rental concessions to maintain target occupancy levels.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on growth sustainability, capital allocation, credit trends, and margin guidance, with a generally positive but probing tone focused on underlying risk and earnings durability.
  • Management’s sentiment was confident during prepared remarks, with statements like “our strategy, platform, talent and momentum position us to achieve these objectives.” During Q&A, responses remained detailed and constructive, but showed caution on economic uncertainty and credit outlook, as in “We did experience modest linked quarter increase in special mentioned loans…We still felt it prudent to move to 35% to 40%.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts maintained a slightly more positive tone, probing the durability of growth and capital deployment, while management’s remarks shifted from transformation milestones to execution and scale.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from “low double-digit percent growth” for full year 2025 revenue in Q3 to “mid- to high single-digit range” for 2026.
  • Management focus moved from concluding a multi-year transformation to emphasizing execution, scale, and realization of investments.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on loan growth, fee income, credit quality, and capital strategy, but current questions leaned slightly more into the ongoing scalability and risk management aspects.
  • The tone of management has remained confident, but the language indicates a pivot to operational discipline rather than strategic overhaul.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing economic uncertainty as a reason for moderating the 2026 provision outlook.
  • Credit trends, particularly in multifamily commercial real estate, were highlighted as a concern, with rental concessions affecting net operating income.
  • Analysts raised questions about the sustainability of fee growth, capital deployment amid market shifts, and credit risk exposure in select portfolios.

Final Takeaway

Texas Capital concluded 2025 with record results across revenue, net income, and fee income, underscoring the success of its multi-year transformation. Management projects continued positive operating leverage and meaningful earnings growth for 2026, with a focus on mid- to high single-digit revenue growth and expanding fee income streams. The company is emphasizing disciplined execution, operational scale, and risk management as it seeks to sustain industry-leading returns and value creation for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump accuses JPMorgan of political debanking in $5B lawsuit - report
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday is filing a $5B lawsuit against JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and its longtime CEO Jamie Dimon, according to a media report.

This is a developing story. Check back for updates.
